data.title: British American Tobacco: I'm Not Touching This At These Prices
data.description:
Over the past year, British American Tobacco stock (BTI) has undergone a considerable rerating. And by rerating, I basically mean a dramatic expansion of multiples. The P/E for 2026 is 11.9x, and the historical average is much lower than that. Basically, we are seeing this more favorable market sentiment for BAT, and this is reflected not only by the stock's performance and current valuation, but also by points such as Jefferies placing it as a top pick given the “shift toward a smoke-free portfolio.”

But my sentiment is still very negative. The numbers are not so consistent with this optimistic narrative, and in my view, even though the company is good and is making some progress, it still deserves a rating of a company linked to the tobacco industry, dependent on smokeables.

Therefore, my initial rating for BAT is a sell.

BAT’s Valuation

Not long ago, I wrote about Phillip Morris International (PM). Basically, my view is that despite the impressive execution in the transition to smoke-free products, there were several risks in the case, and yet the market priced it as a premium company, practically placing it in the status of a quality compounder, just like Coca-Cola Company (KO) and McDonald's (MCD), since at the time, the forward P/E was above 20x.

The case of BTI stock is not quite the same. The problem here is precisely when we compare the fundamentals that I will mention in the next section with the valuation. Okay, we are still dealing with a stable company, which can be called a cash cow and is trying to make a real shift, but there are too many risks to attribute such a large multiple expansion.

As already mentioned, its multiple is not as rich as PM's, and this is well justified, since PM is at a much more advanced stage of transition. But note how, in several multiples, BTI is already trading at a higher level than Altria (MO), a company that, despite also having a very high dependence on smokeables, carries a more premium status due to its history, geography, premium brands, and very high margins.

But the worst part is when we compare BTI with its own history. We are talking about a stock whose 5-year average P/E ratio is ~8.6x. Therefore, this multiple being at 12.6x (2025) is an increase of more than 40%. Note also that this rerating of the stock has also significantly compressed the dividend yield, which was another strong point of the company. You bought knowing the risk, but at least you had that cash flow up front through a high single-digit dividend yield.

This is one of the points to consider. If the company fails to meet market expectations and continues to deliver more modest results, how much could the drawdown be? If the multiple returns to something close to 10x, we are already talking about a correction of ~20%.

BTI: Only This Growth?

One of the points I thought about when I saw this expansion in valuation was: the company must have made considerable progress in the last year, especially in the direction of transitioning away from dependence on combustibles.

Well, that's not quite the case for BAT. In the consolidated results, revenue in euros rose 1.8%, and the adjusted EPS rose 1.7%. Without much news, the company continued to be what most expected and delivered numbers that it was already delivering before. It’s also worth mentioning that the growth grade is a D-.

The problem for me was “New Category Revenue.” The company called the performance “quality growth” in this segment, but revenue growth was only 2.4% YoY. Giving credit to BTI, the margin improved significantly, and this caused the return on investment to reach 21%, which is already a very satisfactory number. But note in the table below that we are still talking about a new category revenue that still represents only 13% of revenue.

It is interesting that the company is showing that this new part of BTI can be profitable. But for a multiple expansion of this size, I expected to see much more, especially in terms of topline, since a dependence of more than 80% on combustibles still does not remove the main risk from BAT, nor does it change the company's profile.

Other Compelling Points and Other Risks For BAT

Giving the company some credit, there have been significant advances, and this needs to be recognized. For instance, compared to the beginning of 2021, BAT already has three times more consumers for its modern oral products, which currently account for most of the revenue in the new category. In fact, the gross margin for modern oral is already higher than that for combustibles.

In addition, revenue in the United States is returning to a healthier pace, with growth closer to the mid single digits (~4%), and this also has to do with the new revenue category, where some brands, such as Velo, grew 384% and gained almost 7 points of market share.

All of this needs to be recognized, since if the company manages to maintain smokeables at a reasonable pace, it may even be with 0% growth, but if it manages to gradually make this transition and dominate a share of the heated products and oral products market, it is already possible to envision shareholder value.

But at the same time, we have to remember that there are other risks besides valuation and an occasional multiple derating.

For example, this recent advance in the US, especially for new products, depends heavily on how this industry will develop, and the rules can change at any moment. The market should continue to mature in regulatory terms (especially for vapor products).

This also applies to the other players mentioned. Altria already has a very strong presence in the US, and competition here is certain. All of these peers are trying to transition to smoke-free products and win over new audiences, and the market is still in its early stages, so there is no strong guarantee that BAT will continue to gain market share, nor that it will achieve such good margins in a sustainable manner, both due to possible legislative changes and competition.

A second point is precisely the prospects for this market. Although smoke-free products have much better prospects than combustible products, it is still a market that I, personally, have my doubts about. It is a market with some growth potential, with an increase in the number of people who want to quit smoking and are looking for an alternative, but I do not think it is such a strong cultural trend to say that there will be secular growth, or even sustainable growth in this industry in the coming decades.

The Bottom Line

Considering all this, I don't think BAT is a bad company, but even so, my rating is a sell. It is not a sell rating that means I consider it interesting to short the stock, but rather a rating that shows that at this moment of more stretched valuation, uncertain future, and still timid growth, I prefer to avoid it. It does not seem like a good time to have it in the portfolio, and after this rerating, I would prefer to take profits.

data.title: SentinelOne: Competition Seems Fierce
data.description:
After going public in 2021, SentinelOne, Inc.'s (S) bull run was short lived. Fast forward a few years and the stock is miles below the IPO price of $35. Just in the past 52 weeks, there has been declines of nearly 40% and so judging by the stock price there is either something fundamentally wrong with the company or the valuation has corrected significantly. SentinelOne reported their latest earnings last month and so today I'll be initiating coverage with a look at their results among other things.

Below, it is shown that SentinelOne is creating valuable AI-powered cybersecurity solutions. However, with top line growth being on a clear downtrend and gross margin seeing some pressures, I believe that fierce competition in this space is weighing on their performance even if the TAM is large. Soft Q4 guidance is potentially another reflection of this. With the valuation having come down but still at a premium to the IT sector, I would say that SentinelOne is around fairly valued right now and is a hold.

Purple AI Drives Better Outcomes

Before looking at their financial results, let's take a look at how AI is really driving value creation. One of SentinelOne's key offerings is Purple AI and in the above infographic it is shown that client outcomes have been improved significantly. Figures like 60% reduced chance of major security incidents and 55% quicker security threat mitigation shows that their core AI solutions are creating very real value. As a result, 3 year ROI for customers is reportedly a whopping 338%. In today's age of cybersecurity threats, incidents can wipeout large amounts of profit or capital and so the value proposition of advanced AI tools, like the ones SentinelOne create, is likely robust. At the end of the day, the value a company brings to the table is highly important and in that regard I would say they seem pretty well positioned.

Singularity Platform Offerings

In terms of their Singularity Platform as a whole, there is widespread implementation of AI in their solutions as well. As stated above, this includes generative AI and agentic AI. You can also see that they have added new solutions to AI & Automation, Data, and Threat Services. This expansion in portfolio is likely in response to expanding and evolving customer needs and so I would say that they have been proven to be innovators in this space.

Market Size Offers Opportunity

Lastly, quickly before we get into their Q3 results, let's talk about the size of the market. As shown above, the TAM is currently over $100 billion with areas like data analytics leading the way. Given the size of the company right now and the rate at which they are pushing into AI cybersecurity, I would say that they have the potential to capitalize on significant opportunities moving forward. The estimates above are 2025 forecasts and given the trend of cybersecurity incidents, I believe the TAM has a good likelihood of further expanding in the upcoming years.

Q3 Growth A Little Sluggish

For FY2026 Q3, SentinelOne generated total revenues of $259 million and that represents a growth rate of 23%. While this YoY increase isn't bad per se, you can see in the above chart that top line growth has overall been on a downtrend for quite a while.

Even though some slowdown is excusable due to the larger growth base of the business, I do find the relative sluggishness to be somewhat worrying. This could be a reflection of stiff competition in this space. With the amount of effort they are putting into AI, you'd probably expect better business activity and so there could be some disappointment. In terms of performance versus analyst expectations, they were able to beat by a razor thin margin of $2.73 million to show some very mild outperformance in the quarter.

Lastly, ARR is usually a better measure of longer term business momentum but in Q3, the growth rate for this metric was also 23%. This is a slowdown from the previous quarter's 24% ARR increase and so again, there seems to be some sluggishness for SentinelOne.

Customer Metrics

While there is no doubt that there is some sluggishness on their top line, customer metrics show adoption of their solutions have continued at a respectable pace. There was a 20% increase in $100K+ ARR customers YoY and customers that have adopted 3+ or 4+ solution categories have shown a large increase in the past 24 months. In my view, the considerable growth in customers that have adopted multiple solutions suggests that SentinelOne's value proposition does remain strong even if revenue growth could be better.

Margins Largely Improve

Overall, SentinelOne's margins are in good positions. However, it should be noted that while a gross margin of 79% is still exceptionally high, it does represent a bit of contraction from the year ago quarter. This is potentially another sign that competition in this space is quite fierce. Let's face it. Currently, there's no shortage of companies offering next-gen AI cybersecurity solutions and while SentinelOne's products are providing great value, I believe the relatively large amount of competitors is denting their growth and probably their gross margin as well.

In terms of the other figures above, operating margin jumped into positive territory as a sign of good corporate effectiveness and the sizeable jump in FCF margin is an indication that cash generation is looking good. For the bottom line, SentinelOne managed an adjusted EPS of $0.07 which beat expectations by $0.02.

Government Contracts

Earlier this month, it was reported that SentinelOne received GovRAMP authorization at the High Impact Level. This is really quite a big deal as it means that the company can now enter into contracts with U.S. federal and state agencies. This of course can lead to better growth moving forward. Andrew Howell, SentinelOne's VP of Government Affairs, stated that

Achieving GovRAMP Authorization at the High Impact level is a significant milestone because this opens the doors for state and local government organizations to deploy state-of-the-art security, which provides better protection for the most critical data and infrastructure.

While near term financial impact isn't likely to be significant, I believe this development does allow for more opportunities down the road. This should add to the TAM size we discussed earlier and so now it's likely a matter of execution.

Q4 Guidance Is Soft

We'll focus on Q4 guidance with just one quarter to go for the fiscal year but it should be mentioned that SentinelOne didn't really raise full year expectations despite beating on both the top and bottom line. The $1.001 billion expected for revenue is slightly above the previously provided midpoint but the 78.5% for gross margin is at the lower end of the range provided back in Q2. The 3% expectation for operating margin is unchanged.

For Q4, they are projecting revenue growth of 20% YoY. That would be a deceleration from Q3's already relatively sluggish growth rate and so there should be some concerns regarding business activity. As for gross margin, they are expecting 77.5%. That would be a notable dip from the prior year period's 79% and so the potential competitive pressures discussed earlier are expected to continue. Lastly, operating margin is expected to be at 5%. This implies around 4 percentage points of expansion and so at least their corporate efficiency is likely to be solid. As a whole, I consider Q4 guidance to be a bit soft as the continued top line deceleration and gross margin contraction are usually trademark signs of stiff competition. Post-earnings, analysts also considered the outlook to be soft.

Valuation Around Fair

As shown in the chart above, SentinelOne's forward P/S ratio has been on a slide since late 2024. While Seeking Alpha shows that the stock trades at a 32% premium to the IT sector in terms of this multiple, I believe the 4.852 reading shows the valuation is around fair.

In today's article, I found a company that's offering innovative and competitive AI-powered cybersecurity solutions. This should be able to justify quite a high valuation. Why is the valuation contraction justified then? Well, even though they are competitive, this space itself seems to a highly competitive one and you can see that from SentinelOne's slowing growth and gross margin pressure.

In my view, a premium to the IT sector is also justified as the TAM is large and the door is now open for them to pursue government contracts. Therefore, I would say that the valuation is much more reasonable than it has been and is a good reflection of both the opportunity and challenges the company faces.

Recap

To sum it up, SentinelOne is an innovative company that provides value-creating solutions in a highly competitive space. The problem with that is despite a large market, growth is slowing at a potentially worrying pace. At this point, due to a combination of factors discussed in this article, I view SentinelOne's long term prospects in a cautiously optimistic manner and I believe that the valuation currently captures that correctly. Therefore, I have decided to initiate the stock at a hold rating.

data.title: Exail Technologies: Europe's Defense Autonomy Push Creates A Compelling Buy
data.description:
European aerospace and defense stocks are in focus, driven by an increased need to increase European security autonomy supported by expanding defense budgets. In a prior report, I covered TKMS, which is a market leader in conventional submarines. In this report I am adding Exail Technologies (EXALF) to my coverage.

What Exail Technologies Does

Exail Technologies was formed in 2022 when two French technology companies, namely ECA Group and iXblue, merged. ECA Group was founded in 1936 and focused on autonomous underwater and marine robotics, while iXblue, founded in 2000, specializes in navigation and photonics. The merger brought two complementary technology portfolios together at a time when defense weapon systems and capabilities are rapidly being modernized.

The company is active in three main areas:

  • Autonomous Systems & Robotics: Provides underwater drones, unmanned surface vessels, and mine countermeasure platforms.
  • Navigation & Sensors: Provides inertial navigation systems and motion sensors with applications in the land, sea, air, and space domains.
  • Photonics & Aerospace: Provides optical components and fiber-optic technologies as well as communication systems and space instrumentation.

The core of the business is autonomous naval vehicles and navigation systems, but we are increasingly seeing demand across other domains increasing. So, the company is an aerospace and defense company focusing on providing a technological edge in autonomy, intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance, as well as navigation and fiber optic solutions. The company has seen its order intake surge from €220 million in 2021 to €612 million by H1 2025, with more orders expected for 2025. Revenues during the first quarter were €220 million, indicating 35% growth while order intake surged 279%. It shows that the company has a growing revenue path ahead.

The Risks and Opportunities for Exail

As a French company focused on robotics, autonomy, navigation, and photonics technologies, Exail is positioned well to benefit from increasing defense budgets. The crisis regarding Greenland seemingly has been defused for the time being. However, at the same time, the comments we heard at the World Economic Forum indicate that power balances are set to shift. Europe will have to step up its game, and it is already doing so, and the crisis in Greenland only amplified the need for that. New technology capabilities are critical for improving the European security and defense capabilities.

At the same time, we should also be mindful about the risks. Not all companies are in the same stage to handle increased demand from the defense market. The major risk at this point is simply the dependence on defense spending cycles. In 2024, Exail derived 56% of revenues from defense clients and 44% from civil clients. I believe that in the years ahead, the share of defense clients will substantially increase. That will increase the dependence on defense contracts. That is marked as the highest risk in the risk matrix, and that is generally a good thing since it is also the biggest opportunity at this time.

At the same time, we note that as an enabler through technology, the company has to continue investments to maintain and expand technological leadership, and due to the high-technology nature of the company’s solutions, export controls may apply.

There is no real risk mitigation for that other than the company being able to ramp up volumes and revenues in the years ahead that should show that risks are managed and spread well.

Exail Technologies Initiates With A Buy Rating

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Note: Price targets are based on Exail’s listing at the Euronext Paris. Price targets are converted from EUR to USD.

Exail Technology stock is definitely not cheap when we consider the company’s median EV/EBITDA of 11.3x. Given the substantial growth opportunities in aerospace and defense and European companies being somewhat preferred to benefit from the increases in European defense budgets, I do not believe that an 11.3x EV/EBITDA can be justified for a company attempting to push the technology envelope. As a result, I am using the aerospace and defense peer group EV/EBITDA to set the price target. That also makes sense as we see that the company is currently trading more or less in line with the price target commanded by the earnings for 2025. Even when we do not pull in the 2027 earnings into the target window, we still see 48% upside with a $181.09 price target. That is enough to rate the stock a buy.

In terms of growth, we are expecting 23.1% compounded annual growth in sales measured from 2024. On EBITDA, we expect 31.7% growth, while on free cash flow, the compounded growth is less impressive at 4.1%. This is driven by growing capital expenditures and a high free cash flow in 2024, providing a difficult base to benchmark the growth against.

The company is expected to grow sales in parallel with margin expansion from 20.2% to 24.2%, marking an increase of four points. Operating and free cash flow are expected to dip in 2025, followed by a recovery in the years after. From a cash conversion perspective, we also see that 2024 was an outlier. Typically, cash conversion is 35%-40%. CapEx as a share of operating cash flows is set to decrease, but it will remain at elevated levels to support demand. The company is expected to see its net debt decline steadily, and this year, absent of any major M&A activities or capital expenditures beyond what is currently modeled, I expect the net debt to be slightly below the 2x-2.5x that I target for aerospace and defense companies.

Conclusion: Exail Stock Looks Attractive

Exail Technologies appears to be expensive when looking at the current EV/EBITDA or the median EV/EBITDA. However, we note that Europe is in the early innings of substantially growing defense budgets, and while spending will not solely flow to European defense contractors, I believe that we will see European countries select European defense contractors where possible to reduce reliance on the US for security solutions. Companies like Exail Technologies that push the technology envelope are positioned extremely well to capitalize on the current demand trends.

data.title: S&P 500 Snapshot: Sour Start Leads To Weekly Loss
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The S&P 500 kicked off the shortened trading week on a sour note but managed to claw back a portion of those losses, ultimately finishing the week with a loss of -0.4%. The index is now 0.88% off its all-time high from January 12, 2026. Here is a snapshot of the index from the past week:

The table below summarizes the number of record highs reached each year dating back to 2013.

Here is a snapshot of the index from the past six months with a 50-day moving average:

S&P 500: A Perspective on Drawdowns

On October 9, 2007, the S&P 500 reached an all-time high, closing the day at 1565.15. Then on March 9, 2009, the index dropped ~57% off of its high from exactly 17 months before, closing the day at 676.53. This time period became known as the Global Financial Crisis. It took over 5 years before the index reached a new all-time high on March 28, 2013, where it closed out at 1569.19. The chart below is a snapshot of record highs and selloffs since the 2007 peak reached on October 9, 2007.

What happens if we take out the Global Financial Crisis? Here's a snapshot of the same chart above where the start date has been changed to the trough reached on March 9, 2009. Note the recent sell-offs in 2022.

Here are a few tables with the number of days of a 1% or greater change in either direction and the number of days of corrections (down 10% or more from the record high).

And here is a linear chart of the index since October 9, 2007:

Here is a linearly scaled version of the same chart with the 50- and 200-day moving averages. The index has been above the 50-day moving average since January 20th and above the 200-day moving average since May 12th. Additionally, the 50-day moving average has been above the 200-day moving average since July 1st.

S&P 500: A Perspective on Volatility

For a sense of the correlation between the closing price and intraday volatility, the chart below overlays the S&P 500 since 2007 with the intraday price range. On April 9th, the index experienced its largest intraday price volatility (10.77%) since December 24th, 2018 (19.10%). Also included is the 20-day moving average to identify trends in volatility. Over the past 20 days, the average percent change from the intraday low to the intraday high is 0.70%.

S&P 500 versus S&P Equal Weight

The S&P 500 is a market cap-weighted index which includes roughly the 500 largest U.S. stocks spanning 11 sectors. The S&P 500 Equal Weight Index includes the same constituents as the S&P 500, but each company is equally weighted at a fixed weight. So how do these two indexes match up against each other this year?

The S&P 500 is currently up 1.02% year-to-date, while the S&P Equal Weight is up 3.71% year-to-date.

ETFs associated with the S&P 500 include: iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV), SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO), SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (SPYM), and Invesco S&P 500® Equal Weight ETF (RSP).

data.title: Investment Risk Is Underappreciated
data.description:
Market Brief – The “Greenland” Impact

This week’s markets were driven by headline risk, economic uncertainty, and the early innings of earnings season. With the markets closed last Monday for the Martin Luther King holiday, U.S. equities sold off sharply on Tuesday. President Trump’s tariff threats against key European allies, tied to a controversial push for Greenland, triggered that selloff across global equity markets. The S&P 500 dropped more than 2%, while the Dow and Nasdaq logged their worst single‑day percentage losses in three months, stoking fears of renewed trade conflicts just as markets were trying to stabilize after year‑end weakness. European equities slid sharply, with major indices such as Germany’s DAX and France’s CAC 40 falling over 1% amid the tariff shock.

However, that narrative shifted sharply on Wednesday as President Trump addressed the world at the annual “Davos Confab,” where the elite mingle to decide the fate of the world. The White House later confirmed that the scheduled tariffs on several European nations would not proceed. Furthermore, Reuters reported that President Trump said a “framework of cooperation” had been reached regarding Greenland, and tariff plans set for February were nixed. That pivot sparked a relief rally, with the S&P 500 climbing over 1% on Wednesday, its largest single‑day percentage gain in several weeks, as all 11 sectors advanced, led by energy stocks.

Notably, earnings season ramped up, with reports from many notable companies delivering mixed signals. Investors showed little tolerance for anything short of strong results and clear forward guidance.

  • Netflix (NFLX) added 13.1 million subscribers, beating estimates, but the stock dropped over 6% after pausing its buyback program and issuing soft Q1 guidance.
  • Procter & Gamble (PG) missed on revenue and reported sluggish volume growth, especially in grooming and fabric care, as sticky input costs weighed on margins.
  • Kinder Morgan (KMI) met earnings expectations but missed revenue targets, citing lower natural gas volumes and a softer demand outlook. The stock drifted lower amid market questioning of midstream energy’s 2026 growth story.
  • Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) posted strong pharmaceutical sales and raised its full-year guidance, helping the stock hold gains.

Across the board, results made it clear that high valuations leave little room for error. Markets want clean beats, firm guidance, and evidence of margin control.

Economic data remained in focus ahead of the Federal Reserve’s late‑January meeting. GDP came in at 4.4% above estimates, and while the headline was strong, it remains a function of the ongoing tariff battle and its impact on net exports. Furthermore, very few mentioned the boost to GDP from an enormous drawdown in personal savings.

In brief, market behavior reflected a tug‑of‑war between headline noise and economic fundamentals. Geopolitical risk reprieve provided short‑term support, but lingering inflation and policy uncertainty keep volatility elevated. Investors now pivot toward more earnings next week from many of the “Magnificent 7,” beginning with Microsoft, Meta, and Tesla on Wednesday, followed by Apple on Thursday.

Which brings us to the market.

Technical Backdrop – Bulls Remain In Control

This week’s price action reflected growing short‑term volatility and technical signals that traders use to gauge potential risk and reward. After a politically driven selloff early in the week, market internals weakened before recovering mid‑week. The CBOE Volatility Index (VIX) surged above 20.0 on Tuesday, then quickly retraced back toward 17.0 by Friday, highlighting swings in fear and complacency among traders. A high, then rapid drop in VIX suggests short‑term traders may be reducing hedges after the headline shock passed, but volatility could remain elevated in the weeks ahead. As MarketWatch observed, “a recent spike in the fear gauge was swiftly erased after tariff threats were softened,” yet analysts warn that sustained volatility remains likely.

Breadth measures are also telling a mixed story. Major indexes like the S&P 500 and Nasdaq closed the week with only modest net movement, but many breadth indicators remain tepid. Recent market breadth data showed that a smaller proportion of S&P 500 stocks are making new highs even as the index approaches record levels, signaling a more narrow leadership. This pattern often precedes a larger corrective phase if broader participation does not improve, and recent analysis pointed to ongoing breadth weakness despite headline strength.

Rotation is evident beneath the surface. Small‑cap stocks and cyclical sectors have outperformed as of late, pushing the Russell 2000 to a series of record highs and outperforming megacaps year‑to‑date. However, small-caps are now very overbought and extended, suggesting a near-term rotational risk is becoming more likely. Furthermore, this shift away from the largest, momentum tech names, which generate a significant share of earnings growth, to more economically and non-profitable companies is a risk if economic “reflation” fails to mature. Such a rotation into small capitalization companies can be constructive, but only if it broadens rather than temporarily lifts market averages.

With the market closing at 6,915 on Friday, here are key technical support and resistance levels to watch going into next week:

Resistance Levels

• Primary resistance: 7,000 — First initial resistance following any attempt to break out to new all-time highs.• Secondary resistance: 7,100 — First initial Fibonacci extension level.• Extended resistance: 7,200 — Third Fibonacci extension level

Support Levels

• Primary support: 6,913 — 20-day moving average.• Secondary support: 6,836 — 50‑day moving average and recent lows.• Key support: 6,378 — 100-day moving average

These levels are critical guides. A breakout to new all-time highs on strong volume could signal continuation of the broader uptrend, whereas a break below 6,836 would shift short‑term momentum toward deeper correction risk. Traders should continue to monitor portfolio risk and rebalance as needed until the market provides better direction with a trend in one direction or the other.

Key Catalysts Next Week

The market arrives at a turning point as January wraps up into a week that will define near‑term risk appetite. Volatility remains elevated, and the narrative has shifted decisively from political headline swings to earnings and economic fundamentals. Last week’s tariff tensions triggered sharp moves across major tech names and drove the CBOE Volatility Index back above 20 before it retraced. Investors now turn to hard data and earnings from key bellwethers on whether the growth and inflation story supporting 2026 valuations can hold up. The backdrop is a market pricing in expectations of continued strength in consumer and business demand, but also significant sensitivity to guidance and Fed policy signals.

The Fed’s January meeting on the 28th looms large as a pivotal catalyst. With inflation still above target and short‑term yields higher, any shift in language on rate cuts or growth expectations could move stocks and bonds sharply. Early-week economic reports, such as durable goods orders and consumer confidence, will set the tone, but it is the big tech earnings mid‑week that have traders bracing for impact. Microsoft (MSFT), Tesla (TSLA), Apple (AAPL), and Meta Platforms (META) are reporting results that will deeply influence the narrative on sector rotation, growth sustainability, and the pricing of risk assets. Stocks with wide exposure to consumer demand, cloud and AI adoption, and discretionary spending will be heavily scrutinized, with guidance statements likely to matter more than quarterly beats or misses. Given the market’s recent sensitivity to earnings guidance, any signs of softening demand or caution in capex could quickly alter the technical landscape.

Investment Risk Is Underappreciated

There’s an old parable worth remembering, especially when it comes to investment risk and the markets. Once upon a time, there was a young shepherd who was tasked with watching over a flock of sheep. Eventually, bored and craving attention, he runs into the village shouting, “Wolf!” The villagers drop everything to help, only to find there’s no danger. He laughs, amused by everyone’s reaction. Later, he does it again. Once again, the villagers fall for it. Unfortunately, when a wolf does indeed appear, he cries for help. But not wanting to be fooled again, the villagers ignore him, and the flock is quickly slaughtered.

The lesson is simple: lie often enough, and no one believes you when it matters. In investing, we see this regularly with pundits and YouTubers who continually claim that market crashes and devastation are imminent as the bull market continues its climb. Eventually, those individuals get tuned out. It is the same with permabulls who see upside in every dip. When the real turning point comes, few are listening. That’s how risk blindsides the crowd.

The financial markets do one thing very well: lull investors into a false sense of security and complacency. Rallies stretch longer than logic allows, optimism builds on hope rather than data, and eventually risk becomes ignored, just as the boy who “cried wolf.” Today, investors are leaning hard into a bullish narrative, while ignoring key warning signs, from slowing earnings to inflated valuations. The market’s investment risk profile is rising, but few are listening.

Earnings and Profit Margin Expectations Are Very Optimistic

Recent quarters have delivered strong earnings surprises, but much of that upside came from aggressive cost-cutting and financial engineering rather than organic revenue growth. We can see this in the breakdown of earnings between accounting gimmicks and actual revenue growth.

As shown below, Wall Street is increasingly confident of earnings growth in 2026, with a faster growth rate than in any of the last several years.

However, consumer demand is softening, input costs are sticky, savings rates are declining, and employment remains weak. Furthermore, the post-pandemic pricing power that companies once enjoyed is now fading. That certainly isn’t the recipe for a rather exuberant surge in earnings, particularly when earnings are already very deviated above the historical growth trend.

Despite the obvious risk, investors are treating earnings as a certainty rather than a variable. However, that assumption breaks down quickly when growth slows and margins compress. Currently, many Wall Street analysts have been slow to lower earnings estimates. While that is typical, the investment risk to individuals is that by the time revisions hit the wires, markets have already adjusted. This lag creates a false sense of security. Therefore, if earnings begin to disappoint in the next few quarters, stocks priced for perfection will get punished.

The same analysis applies to corporate profit margins, which expanded massively during the stimulus-driven recovery. However, despite the reversal of that stimulus, Wall Street expects profit margins to surge to record levels in 2026. While during the pandemic, companies could raise prices while holding labor and input costs low, that cycle is reversing. Wage inflation remains sticky, and commodity costs (silver, copper, and other metals) are surging. With companies now competing more on price again, profit margins are an investment risk that shouldn’t be dismissed.

While the bullish consensus is, well, very bullish, companies can’t maintain wide margins if they can’t pass along cost pressures. Is this guaranteed to happen? No. However, investors betting on sustained profitability could be caught offside given that the markets are priced for perfection. Of all the investment risks, this is one of the clearest red flags being ignored right now.

The Reflation Narrative Is Built on Assumptions

As I discussed at the 2026 Investment Summit last weekend, the prevailing market narrative is simple: inflation is falling, the economy is growing, and central banks will continue to cut rates. In other words, 2026 is a year of “reflation.” However, none of these assumptions is guaranteed. Inflation is proving sticky, central banks are closer to pausing rate cuts, and, most importantly, economic growth is currently a function of one-off issues, as David Rosenberg recently noted.

Furthermore, the labor market remains tight, and consumer spending is uneven, with the top 10% of income earners accounting for nearly 50% of spending.

While markets tend to move ahead of policy, the investment risk arises when policy doesn’t match the forecast. If the reflation narrative takes hold and economic growth increases, inflation will likely rise. Such would put the Federal Reserve in a difficult position. If the Fed pauses, or worse, lifts rates as a response, the equity risk premium shrinks. That means valuations must adjust downward, and investors believing in a smooth landing and a quick pivot to rate cuts are leaning too far into hope.

Valuations Are Still Stretched

Of course, this ties back to the one investment risk that is well known, and currently ignored: Valuations. Valuations are the “boy who cried wolf.” For several years, Wall Street, YouTubers, and the media have all decried valuations as expensive, predicting an imminent correction. Unfortunately, valuations don’t work that way. Now, after several years of false alarms, investors feel valuations are “the boy who cried wolf.”

However, valuations are the one investment risk that should not be ignored, but understood. As discussed previously, valuations are a terrible timing indicator, but rather a measure of sentiment in the near term. The current trailing twelve-month price-to-earnings ratio sits at 26, near historic extremes. The Shiller CAPE ratio, which adjusts for inflation and smooths cycles over a decade, stands near 39. Forward P/E estimates for 2026 earnings are in the 23 range. By almost every measure, equities are priced at levels that historically limit future returns.

However, this is also the investment risk that investors need to prepare for. At current valuation levels, stocks don’t need a crisis to fall; they only need disappointment. If growth falls short, or if the Fed doesn’t deliver the cuts the market expects, equities face pressure. In other words, a “recession” is not the risk; it is just anything that is “less than perfect.”

The Risks Are Interconnected

Each of these risks, earnings, margins, the reflation narrative, and valuations feeds into the others. If earnings disappoint, margin assumptions fall apart. If inflation re-accelerates, the Fed stays hawkish, hurting valuations. These feedback loops increase the risk to investors, leaving them exposed to negative outcomes. With the market currently built on “narratives,” rather than fundamentals, when the story changes, price will follow. It’s not a matter of if sentiment will shift, but when. Therefore, positioning yourself ahead of that shift is critical.

There’s no magic bullet, but there are steps you can take to reduce risk without giving up opportunity.

  1. Diversify across asset classes. That means holding stocks, bonds, and cash.
  2. Tilt your equity exposure toward quality. Look for companies with strong balance sheets, consistent earnings, and pricing power.
  3. Reduce exposure to over-valued names. Focus on valuation. History tells us that buying “cheap” protects capital in downturns.
  4. Use defensive sectors like healthcare and consumer staples as ballast.
  5. Review your position sizes. Don’t let a few names dominate your portfolio.
  6. Lastly, raise some cash. Not as a market call, but as a tool to take advantage of lower prices later.

Most importantly, the key to navigating investment risks this year will be remaining data-driven. Ignore the headlines, disregard narratives, and watch real indicators like earnings revisions, margin trends, inflation reports, and Fed guidance. Don’t forecast. React to changes as they occur.

Risk doesn’t disappear. Investors ignore it at their peril. If you want to survive the next market shock, prepare now. Hope is not a strategy. Discipline is.

Market Statistics & Analysis

Weekly technical overview across key sectors, risk indicators, and market internals

Market & Sector X-Ray: Pullback To Support

As noted last week, 2026 has started with a massive surge in the “economic reflation” story. Basic Materials and Energy are up over 10% since the beginning of the year. Staples, an area that was hated last year, has also risen as investors expect a resurgence in economic activity. These sectors are now very overbought, so profit-taking and rebalancing make sense.

Technical Composite: 74.58 – Weakness Appears

The overall technical condition remains bullish, but has weakened slightly over the last month. After hitting more extreme overbought conditions heading into October, the market has continued to consolidate, working off some of that state. While not bearish, some further weakness early next week would be unsurprising, providing a better entry point for a rally at some point.

Fear/Greed Index: 81.26 – Extreme Greed Remains

Positioning in equities remained strong this week, despite the Tuesday selloff. Retail investors stepped in and bought broad market exposure as the market rallied back above near-term support. With the “economic reflation” narrative gaining traction across sectors, investors remain extremely bullish, with allocations to equities at historically high levels.

Relative Sector Performance

This week saw volatility on Tuesday, but it swiftly reversed as markets moved higher into the end of the week. Transportation, Materials, and Energy are the most overbought, suggesting investors should consider rebalancing risk exposures accordingly. Financials are the most oversold on a relative basis, and offer a potential catch point for any near-term rotation.

Most Oversold Sector Holdings

As noted above, the most oversold sector is Financials. BRK.B, MA, V, and JPM are currently the most out-of-favor in the sector, with JPM offering a more compelling opportunity given its positioning in the financial sector overall.

Sector Model & Risk Ranges

As noted above, the “reflation trade” marked the beginning of the new year. The sharp advance in Basic Materials, Industrials, Energy, Transportation, Small and Mid-Cap, and Gold and Gold Miners has sent deviations from long-term means toward extremes that are ultimately unsustainable. However, these extremes can persist for a while, so it is important to maintain exposure while also managing risk along the way. Price changes across numerous sectors and factors are well outside of norms. Trade accordingly.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Precious Metals Royalty And Streaming Companies - December 2025 Report
data.description:
Precious metals royalty and streaming companies represent a very interesting sub-industry of the precious metals mining industry. They provide some leverage to the growing metals prices, similar to the typical mining companies; however, they are less risky in comparison to them. Their incomes are derived from royalty and streaming agreements. Under a metal streaming agreement, the streaming company provides an upfront payment to acquire the right to future deliveries of a predefined percentage of the metal production of a mining operation.

The streaming company also pays some ongoing payments that are usually well below the market price of the metal. They can be set as a fixed sum (e.g., $300/toz gold) or as a percentage (e.g., 20% of the prevailing gold price), or a combination of both (e.g., the lower of a) $300/toz gold and b) 20% of the prevailing gold price). The royalties usually apply to a small fraction of the mining project production (usually 1-3%), and they are not connected with ongoing payments. They can have various forms, but the most common is a small percentage of the net smelter return ("NSR"). The NSR is calculated as revenues from the sale of the mined products minus transportation and refining costs.

To better track the overall performance of the whole sub-industry, I created a capitalisation-weighted index (the Precious Metals Royalty and Streaming Index) consisting of 11 companies (in June 2020, expanded to 15). Later, based on the inquiries of readers, I also introduced an equal-weighted version of the index. Until March 2021, both indices included the same companies and were calculated back to January 2019.

However, some major changes occurred in April 2021. Due to the boom of the royalty and streaming industry and the emergence of many new companies, the indices experienced two major changes. First of all, the market capitalisation-weighted index was modified to include only the 5 biggest companies: Franco-Nevada (FNV), Wheaton Precious Metals (WPM), Royal Gold (RGLD), Osisko Gold Royalties (OR), and Sandstorm Gold (SAND). The combined weight of these 5 companies on the old index was around 95%, therefore, the small companies had only a negligible impact on their performance. The values of the index were recalculated back to January 2019, and between January 2019 and March 2021, the difference in the overall performance of the old and the new index was only 2.29 percentage points.

The previous editions of the monthly report can be found here: May 2019, June 2019, July 2019, August 2019, September 2019, October 2019, November 2019, December 2019, January 2020, February 2020, March 2020, April 2020, May 2020, June 2020, July 2020, August 2020, September 2020, October 2020, November 2020, December 2020, January 2021, February 2021, March 2021, April 2021, May 2021, June 2021, July 2021, August 2021, September 2021, October 2021, November 2021, December 2021, January 2022, February 2022, March 2022, April 2022, May 2022, June 2022, July 2022, August 2022, September 2022, October 2022, November 2022, December 2022, January 2023, February 2023, March 2023, April 2023, May 2023, June 2023, July 2023, August 2023, September 2023, October 2023, November 2023, December 2023, January 2024, February 2024, March 2024, April 2024, May 2024, June 2024, July 2024, August 2024, September 2024, October 2024, November 2024, December 2024, January 2025, February 2025, March 2025, April 2025, May 2025, June 2025, July 2025, August 2025., September 2025, October 2025, November 2025.

In December, Wheaton Precious Metals' market capitalisation exceeded $50 billion. The second biggest precious metals R&S company, Franco-Nevada, ended December with a market capitalisation of nearly $40 billion. At the bottom of the ranking was Star Royalties (STRFF) with a market capitalisation slightly below $20 million. Silver Crown Royalties (SCRI:CA) outgrew Star by slightly more than $1 million. However, several more movements occurred in the ranking. Metalla Royalty and Streaming (MTA) outgrew Versamet Royalties (VRMTF), Vox Royalty (VOXR) outgrew Vizsla Royalties (VROY:CA), and Nations Royalty (NRC:CA) outgrew Orogen Royalties (OGNNF) and Empress Royalty (EMPYF).

Out of the 16 companies, 12 recorded a share price growth in December. However, the four declines were only negligible. The biggest one experienced Viszla Royalties. Its share price declined by 1.23%. The highest gains enjoyed shareholders of Silver Crown Royalties, whose share price grew by more than 34%. Nations Royalty experienced a more than 30% growth as well. In both cases, the strong growth cannot be attributed to any company-specific news.

In December, gold and silver prices continued to rise. Therefore, the share prices of the SPDR Gold Trust ETF (GLD) and the iShares Silver Trust ETF (SLV) grew by 2.17% and 25.8% respectively. At the same time, the VanEck Vectors Gold Miners ETF (GDX) grew by 3.05%, and the VanEck Vectors Junior Gold Miners ETF (GDXJ) grew by 4.12%. The precious metals R&S companies did better, as the Precious Metals R&S Index increased by 3.98%, and the Precious Metals R&S Equally Weighted Index increased by 7.7%.

The December News

The month of December was relatively poor in news. No major deals took place, only Gold Royalty announced a $70 million acquisition of the Pedra Branca royalty.

OR Royalties (OR) renewed its normal course issuer bid. The company can acquire and cancel up to 9,399,294 shares (approximately 5% of issued and outstanding shares) between December 12, 2025, and December 11, 2026.

On December 16, OR Royalties announced that it repurchased and cancelled 920,632 common shares in 2025. The company also provided some portfolio updates. The most important news is that Ramelius Resources' (RMLRF) Dalgaranga mine is on track to start production soon (OR Royalties holds a 1.44% Gross Revenue Royalty, and that SolGold (SLGGF) and its Cascabel copper project (OR holds a 0.6% NSR royalty and a 6% gold stream) will be acquired by Jiangxi Copper (JIAXF). Moreover, Goldfields' (GFI) Windfall gold mine should start gold production in H1 2029 (OR holds a 2-3% NSR royalty).

Elemental Royalty (ELE) executed an option agreement with First Quantum Minerals (FQVLF). First Quantum will be able to acquire 100% of the Hachita project by paying Elemental $800,000 in total, and expending $6 million on exploration over the next 5 years. Elemental will subsequently retain a 3% NSR royalty. Elemental will also be entitled to advanced annual royalty payments of $100,000 per year, and milestone payments of $3 million in total.

Versamet Royalties (VRMTF) graduated from the TSX Venture Exchange to the Toronto Stock Exchange, effective December 10, 2025.

Gold Royalty (GROY) acquired the producing Pedra Branca royalty. Based on the agreement, Gold Royalty is entitled to 25% NSR royalty on gold and 2% NSR royalty on copper produced at BHP Group's (BHP) Pedra Branca mine in Brazil. The royalty should be generating cash flows of around 2,800 toz of gold equivalent per year. Gold Royalty agreed to pay $70 million for this royalty.

To finance the abovementioned transaction, Gold Royalty made a $103.5 million bought deal financing. The company issued 25,875,000 shares at a price of $4 per share.

Vox Royalty (VOXR) acquired a 1.5% NSR royalty on the feasibility-stage Stockman copper-gold-zinc-silver project operated by Aeris Resources (ARSRF). The royalty should be able to generate a cash flow of around $3 million per year. Vox agreed to pay A$15 million ($10 million) for this asset.

Vizsla Royalties (VROY:CA) graduated to the OTCQX Best Market, effective December 10, 2025.

Sailfish Royalty (SROYF) declared a quarterly dividend of $0.0125 per share. It will be paid on January 15, 2026, to shareholders of record as of December 31, 2025.

Orogen Royalties (OGNNF) announced that Kodiak Copper (KDKCF) released an initial resource estimate for its MPD copper-gold project. Orogen's 2% NSR royalty area contains indicated resources of 134 million lb copper and 140,000 toz gold, and inferred resources of 599 million lb copper and 320,000 toz gold.

On December 18, Orogen noted First Majestic Silver's (AG) positive drill results from the Ermitano property, upon which Orogen holds a cash-flowing 2% NSR royalty.

Empress Royalty (EMPYF) released its Q3 financial results. The company recorded revenues of $4.2 million, which represents a new record high. And while the operating cash flow of $0.7 million represents a 75% decline compared to Q2, the net income increased by 27%, to $1.4 million. Empress ended Q4 with cash of $5.5 million, and debt of $3.4 million.

Star Royalties (STRFF) announced the entering into a binding agreement to convert its 2%NSR royalty on the Elk gold mine into a 5% equity stake in Gold Mountain Mining (GMTNF), the operator of the Elk gold mine.

The January Outlook

The month of January has been great so far, with gold prices setting new record highs over $4,700/toz, and silver prices approaching the $100/toz level. This is also reflected by the share prices of the precious metals R&S companies. If the current market sentiment prevails, the first month of 2026 will end up highly positive.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Amerigo Resources: Electro-Copper Boosts Value For The Shareholders
data.description:
The Market Euphoria Suits Amerigo Resources

This article reflects the market euphoria that, in our view, is providing adequate support for Amerigo Resources Ltd. (ARREF) shares on the US OTCQX market. We believe this has rightly reinforced the optimistic expectations for further value appreciation of this Canadian copper and molybdenum concentrate company in Chile. We reaffirm our positive vision of this stock from our previous article dated November 5, 2025.

Here's how we measure current market sentiment, as of this writing: ARREF's share price has risen 262.34% over the past twelve months. Since our last article, it has gained 93.93% (a total return of +98.43% including dividends; this could be even higher considering share buybacks) and outperformed the S&P 500 by 2.12%. Year to date, the share price has risen 26.82%, exceeding the 1% return of S&P 500 Futures (SPX) over the same period.

Our performance benchmark, the S&P 500 futures, closely reflects the performance of the entire US stock market.

About Amerigo Resources Ltd.

In the report “Q4-2025 Operational Results, 2025 Production Target and 2026 Guidance” from Amerigo Resources dated January 13, 2026 (hereinafter referred to as “ Operational Report 2025”), Amerigo states the following about its business:

Amerigo is an innovative copper producer with a long-term relationship with Corporación Nacional del Cobre de Chile (“Codelco”), the world’s largest copper producer.

Amerigo produces copper concentrate and molybdenum concentrate as a by-product at the MVC operation in Chile by processing fresh and historic tailings from Codelco’s El Teniente mine, the world's largest underground copper mine.

An Update Is Needed: Advantages of Investing in Amerigo Resources

As an Amerigo shareholder, you not only benefit from the advantages of copper without investing directly in the physical metal, but you also have the opportunity for higher returns. While past performance is no guarantee of future returns, the trends are favourable for Amerigo and its ARREF stock: The share price has risen by 262.34% in the last twelve months and by 26.82% since the beginning of the year (excluding dividends and share buybacks). By comparison, Copper Futures (HG1:COM) have increased by 34.93% in the last twelve months and by 3.4% since the beginning of the year.

Thanks to its unparalleled electrical and thermal conductivity, copper is an essential component of electrification programs, electromobility, the energy transition, the expansion of AI-powered data centres, and programs to enhance weapons systems for national security.

You are also a shareholder in a company that is optimally leveraging the enormous growth potential of the copper sector. This is the result of a long-standing partnership with Codelco, the world's largest copper producer. Codelco utilises Chilean resources, which are projected to reach 6.13 million tons in 2026—the largest in the world, representing a 3.9% increase year-over-year and a global market share of 27% (according to farmonaut.com). These resources come from the processing of tailings from previous mining activities at the Codelco mine, El Teniente, 22 km northeast of Rancagua, Chile, according to netherlandsandyou.nl,

... the Largest Underground Copper Mine in the World!.

Over 85% of Amerigo's revenue comes from copper sales, while the remaining 15% comes from molybdenum sales.

Amerigo's operating results for 2025, and in particular the copper and molybdenum production forecasts for 2026, are now available. In parallel, analysts have moved forward with their forecasts for copper supply and demand, as well as their potential impact on copper prices in the coming years. This article reports on the expectations of Amerigo Resources shareholders who believe there is reason to hope for dividends and share buybacks, with some of them potentially expecting a significant improvement over 2025.

Our Previous Coverage

In mid-2025, Amerigo Resources faced serious challenges, primarily due to the following events, as reported by Mining Intelligence and News (“ MDO”):

Following a seismic event, activities at the El Teniente copper mine were immediately suspended in early August 2025.

On 10 August 2025, the Labor Directorate authorized Saturday night the partial and gradual restart of underground operations in the Pilar Norte, Esmeralda Panel, Esmeralda, Upper Pacific, Diablo Regimiento, Reno Panel, Dacita, and Reservas Norte sectors, following the authorization already granted by Sernageomin. The shutdown remains in place in Recursos Norte, Andesita, Andes Norte, and Diamante.

Despite this setback, Amerigo demonstrated resilience. Fueled by strong copper price performance, the company recorded a substantial increase in profitability in the third quarter of 2025. This enabled Amerigo to reward its shareholders with a 33% dividend increase. Furthermore, the company was nearing full debt repayment and was thus able to enhance shareholder returns through dividends and share buybacks—particularly given the continued rise in copper prices.

Amerigo's long-standing partnership with the Chilean government, which manages the world's largest copper reserves, adds strength to the company's future prospects. Considering all these factors, Amerigo Resources has proven to be a solid medium- to long-term investment. This assessment is now clearly reflected in the market: the company's share price is climbing very well, signaling growing investor confidence in future growth despite the volatility of the copper market.

Operating Results for Q4 and Full Year 2025

Production at Amerigo Resources is being accelerated, and the company also expects a higher production volume in 2026.

In the Q4 and Full Year 2025 production report dated January 13, 2026, Aurora Davidson, Amerigo’s President and CEO, stated:

MVC delivered its strongest quarterly copper production on record in Q4-2025 and achieved solid annual production, while overcoming adverse operational conditions during the year.

MVC stands for “Minera Valle Central S.A.”, the subsidiary of Amerigo Resources Ltd., which produces copper and molybdenum concentrates in Chile.

Overall, copper and molybdenum production and sales are developing favourably and are positive for the market. This is particularly evident in the second half of 2025, which was an improvement that provided extra positive market sentiment. While processing volumes did fluctuate, these fluctuations were more than offset by copper recovery rates and grades, as these are performing well. The processing of tailings piles, especially historical ones, remains at a robust level, which bodes well with the overall production profile.

Operating costs do fluctuate, too, but despite this volatility, the underlying trend is a general downward trend, which is good.

Production and Profitability Forecast for 2026

In the Q4 and Full Year 2025 production report dated January 13, 2026, Aurora Davidson, Amerigo’s President and CEO, stated:

Our Q4-2025 production also reflects the tangible benefits of the optimization projects implemented last year. These initiatives have quickly delivered measurable improvements, positioning MVC for continued success in this high-copper-price environment. Looking ahead, Amerigo remains focused on sustaining operational excellence and cost performance, rewarding shareholders through the Company’s one-of-a-kind capital return strategy. We look forward to reporting 2025 financial results on February 25, 2026

Amerigo Resources anticipates copper production of 63.8 million pounds and molybdenum production of 1.5 million pounds for 2026. This would mark

the sixth year of increased production guidance, the company stated in the Q4 2025 production report,

despite the planned annual maintenance shutdown in the first quarter of 2026. The company projects an average copper price of $4.80 per pound and a molybdenum price of $21 per pound. The company's cash costs are estimated at $1.98 per pound. The company's official EBITDA forecast for 2026 is $74.5 million.

The company states:

The Company’s projections indicate that each $0.20/lb increase in the copper price could have an average impact of $4.2 million on EBITDA, subject to the determination with Codelco’s El Teniente Division (“DET”) of the DET copper royalty factors for copper prices exceeding $4.80/lb for fresh tailings and $5.50/lb for historic tailings.

Regarding future copper prices, Seeking Alpha recently reported on a study by the consulting firm S&P Global. This study predicts that the rise of artificial intelligence and the defence industry could increase copper demand by 50% by 2040, while supply will not keep pace. Given this fundamental upward trend, further price increases are expected for copper futures (HG1:COM). At the time of writing, they were trading at $5.92 per pound or $13,051 per tonne, nearly 2% upside from $12,800 at the time Seeking Alpha published the S&P Global report. The price of copper has increased by 1.96% since January 15, 2026, to its current price of $5.92 per pound (as of January 23, 2026). Assuming a linear increase of 1.96% every eight days, the average price for the period from January 15, 2026, to January 14, 2027—taking into account the positive impact of AI and defence—would be approximately $8.42 per pound. This estimated average price for the coming year is $2.50 above the current price of $5.92 per pound, representing a growth of 42.23%. This upside appears realistic compared to the previous year's growth of approximately 38% in the price of the red metal.

Our Projection of EBITDA in 2026

Let's assume the average copper price in 2026 is $8.42/lb. Now we determine the projected EBITDA for 2026, under Amerigo's key assumptions and the assumption of a constant linear relationship between the copper price and DET royalties: the DET royalties at a copper price of $4.80/lb would be $1.65/lb (i.e., 34.375%). At a copper price of $8.42/lb, the DET royalties would therefore be approximately $2.89/lb.

Other important assumptions: The molybdenum price remains unchanged. The CLP/USD exchange rate remains unchanged. Cash costs remain unchanged. Copper and molybdenum production levels are those assumed by Amerigo.

The corresponding value for Amerigo's projected EBITDA in 2026 would increase from $74.5 million (Amerigo's forecast) to approximately $130.75 million (our forecast).

Our Projection of FCF in 2026

In the Q4 and Full Year 2025 production report dated January 13, 2026, the company states:

The projected Free Cash Flow (“FCF”) 1 to be generated in 2026, under the Assumptions and Capex, is expected to be $34.8 million (excluding the effect of 2025 settlement adjustments). It is projected that each $0.20/lb increase in the copper price could increase FCF by $4.1 million on average.

Amerigo Resources is forecasting higher free cash flow ("FCF") for 2026 as copper prices continue to trade higher. At a copper price of $4.80/lb, the company estimates FCF of $34.8 million. This is 46.7% of EBITDA.

At an expected copper price of $8.42/lb, we estimated EBITDA to be up to $130.75 million. Applying the same rate of 46.7% to this higher EBITDA gives: $130.75 million × 46.7% = $61.1 million

The expected FCF for 2026 at a copper price of $8.42/lb is $61.1 million compared to the baseline estimate by Amerigo of $34.8 million at $4.80/lb.

Value Creation for The Shareholders

The company states:

The Company repaid $11.5 million in debt (2024: $9.8 million), becoming debt-free in October 2025, and returned $20.3 million to shareholders through dividends and share buybacks during the year (2024: $21.2 million).

The company adds:

Since implementing its CRS in October 2021, Amerigo has returned $98.4 million to shareholders, $67.7 million through quarterly and performance dividends, and $30.7 million through share buybacks, reducing the number of common shares outstanding by 14% compared to the share count at the CRS's inception.

In 2021, approximately 174 million shares were outstanding ("Total Common Shares Outstanding"). 14% of these, or 24.4 million shares, combined with $30.7 million in share buybacks, results in a price of $1.26 per share. If, in the past, 69% was used for quarterly ordinary and performance-related dividends, then approximately $19 million of the $61.1 million in free cash flow projected for 2026 could be used for share buybacks. Amerigo will repurchase up to 11.7 million shares (10% of the public float) as part of a normal share buyback program. The buyback will begin on December 2, 2025, and end on December 1, 2026, with a daily limit of 68,239 shares.

However, given that the share price is 3.5 times higher and is expected to continue to rise due to the bullish sentiment around copper prices and the positive market reaction to Amerigo's CRS, we fear that a buyback of 11.7 million shares is a bit too ambitious. A buyback of approximately 4 to 5 million shares seems more realistic. This would reduce the number of shares outstanding, and the optical effect on dividends per share should still have a bullish effect on the market value of ARREF shares on the US stock market.

Amerigo could leverage its increased cash reserves of $40.3 million (as of January 13, 2026) compared to $28 million in cash and cash equivalents and $3.1 million in tied-up funds (as of September 30, 2025) – a difference of approximately $10 million. The electrification-driven boom in the copper sector is a strong growth driver that, in our view, could allow the company to use these cash reserves, along with free cash flow, to fund the CRS program without major concerns. Amerigo is virtually debt-free.

Also, the company is confident that the $17.5 million investment planned for 2026 will have a positive long-term impact on growth. At a copper price of $4.80 per pound, this investment will pay for itself within a maximum of two years. Since copper prices are expected to rise several notches, we anticipate that the invested capital will be recouped much faster. This will provide Amerigo Resources with more funds in the long term to continue its capital return strategy ("CRS") through dividend payments and share buybacks.

Amerigo Resources Ltd. on the US OTCQX

Amerigo Resources Ltd.'s US OTCQX-listed shares under the symbol “ARREF” were trading at $4.43/share, giving it a market capitalization of $678.40 million, as of this writing.

Technical Analysis

According to Seeking Alpha, the 52-week range is between $1.11 and $4.44. The current share price is 59.6% above the 52-week average of $2.775 per share. The order of the moving averages (20-day, 50-day, and 200-day) is as follows: The 20-day average is above the 50-day average, which in turn is above the 200-day average. This suggests that ARREF stock is not only in a short-term uptrend but also shows signs of a longer-term uptrend.

The RSI reading of 90.24 signals overbought levels, and a bearish impulse could be building up, potentially pushing Amerigo Resources shares down from their current level. Investors may want to wait and see if this represents a healthy price correction before adding to their positions. For market participants wishing to open a position and pursue this strategy, it is riskier, as such a strong upward trend for copper offers no guarantee of a healthy price correction. Also, these overbought levels could still present an opportunity, given the very promising growth prospects for this stock.

Valuation

  • Amerigo Resources offers a Dividend Yield (TTM) of 2.23% compared to the sector median of 1.66%.
  • Amerigo Resources has an EV / EBITDA (FWD) of 8.04 compared to the sector median of 10.06.
  • We forecast free cash flow (FCF) of US$61.1 million for 2026. This results in a market capitalisation-to-FCF ratio of 11.1x, which is slightly more favourable than the sector median of 11.78 (as of today).

The Risk

  • The risk of a possible interruption of copper mining in El Teniente in Chile exists because it is an aseismic area.
  • ARREF shares exhibit low liquidity. Trading data from Seeking Alpha shows an average volume of 177,155 (over three months). This could have negative consequences, as it could make exiting the position more difficult. If investors have to lower the price to sell their shares, they risk weak performance, delays, or even significant losses. The opposite is also possible: low trading volumes can create upward pressure and drive prices higher very fast.
  • There is a risk that copper prices will develop differently from what optimists expect. Analysts at Goldman Sachs are even forecasting a decline in the copper price on the LME (HG1:COM) to $11,000 per tonne by the end of 2026. But this assessment is overshadowed by the vast majority of analysts who remain bullish on copper.

Conclusion

Amerigo Resources Ltd., whose shares trade under the symbol ARREF on the US OTCQX, is an attractive option for investors seeking to capitalise on the growth potential of the copper sector. The company is well-positioned to deliver value to its shareholders through dividend payments and share buybacks. This enhances the stock appeal on the market, where participants are already buying Amerigo shares, driven by rising copper prices, which contribute to the cash flow and profits.
