data.title: Bristol Myers Squibb: The Rerating Was Fast - The Proof Will Be Slow (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
When I first wrote about Bristol Myers Squibb (BMY) in October 2025, the stock was near 5-year lows. My focus was on the Eliquis patent cliff, legacy erosion and execution risks. I saw valuations and dividend offering a decent margin of safety for patient capital. Since then, within 3 months, BMY has delivered a strong ~27% total return including dividends, implying that the easiest rerating part of the thesis has arrived ahead of time and should be largely done.

What has changed since, is higher confidence in the company's forward earnings. The Q3 2025 earnings call and the Analyst Day call highlight growth portfolio momentum and plans to actively replace legacy revenues through CELMoDs, CAR-T expansion and platform reuse. However, much of this translation rests on execution and regulatory milestones. With the valuation reset I expected to capture largely complete and with key proof of execution still ahead, the investment case shifts from a high conviction rerating to a balanced Hold stance. Take some disciplined profits off the table - a ~25% price return is almost 5 years of dividends in a very short term, for context.

Revisiting the Portfolio

On the topline front, Q3 delivered decent growth after a couple of quarters of insipid and negative growth trends. The numbers were encouraging for revenue durability, with the growth portfolio delivering ~16% YTD growth YoY and multiple assets delivering at scale (4 products annualizing at over $1b in the growth portfolio). Eliquis sales grew by ~23% supported by Medicare Part D redesign and improved access. This helped turnaround US growth from negative territory in Q2 to mildly positive Guidance for the full year was revised upward by $750m.

However, legacy erosion is expected to continue at pace, with management guiding a full year decline of 15-17%. Also, it is hard to digest strong Eliquis numbers because it helps the near term cause, while mathematically increasing the height of the eventual cliff (and the magnitude of revenue that will need to be replaced post Loss of Exclusivity). BMY aims to balance this gap by CELMoDs, CAR-T expansion and reusable platforms, but that comes laden with execution and regulatory risks. The Eliquis growth in the US also masks a slower than Q2 international growth level at 6%. Finally, the growth portfolio was already growing at ~18% at the time of my prior thesis, so its continued healthy growth levels are a plus confirming trends, but do not introduce any incremental support from October.

My concerns around some parts of the growth portfolio have not really gone away. Although management has talked about CELMoDs, CAR-T expansion and reusable platforms and that is a viable plan in place there are reasons why I don't consider the risk as having been materially addressed yet. CELMoDs are the cleanest Revlimid successor, but still hinge on regulatory acceptance and sustained uptake. CAR-T can scale meaningfully, but operational complexity limits its ability to fully offset Eliquis sized revenues in the medium term. Opdivo does not have a clear successor and can drag topline by up to $5b on its own. Its revenues now are funding growth in other products, and that is expected to plug the hole - not a concrete execution-ready plan, in my opinion. That is not to say that this gap is unbridgeable - my earlier October projections do show a plausible path, but strategies revealed in the Analyst Day do not do enough to materially reduce risks from where they were in October.

Valuation and Dividend Comfort

Fresh entries that were underwriting the transformation along with a valuation rebound earlier, are left with more of the former risk (which is a multi-year execution path). The forward EV to revenues multiple has now moved to the higher end of the valuation spectrum since 2024, offering little rerating steam ahead. Especially because, while a bridge is being identified to plug the Eliquis LOE gap, the risk remains material enough to limit valuation rerating beyond current levels unless execution clearly backs the strategy.

The dividend remains relatively well covered though. There is no change in that outlook. The payout ratio of (relative to net income) remains below 40%. Even in free cash flow terms, the dividend coverage remains supported. As argued in my earlier thesis, the little over $5b dividend obligations annually gives the improving TTM FCF enough room to shrink before dividends start getting pressured.

Take Profits and Await Fresh Signals

Given the lack of material risk offloading, the recent rerating from the lows is worth forcing disciplined profit taking in my view. The dividends should still keep investor interest alive, but the greater magnitude of execution risks lie ahead than was addressed in Q3 or the Analyst Day call. So, fresh entries should await either a retracement to valuations below ~2.6x or thereabouts to recreate the same opportunity we had in October 2025. Or fresh indicators of risk offloading should emerge. That is not in the shape of Eliquis strength like we saw in Q3, but more around growth elsewhere showing the potential to plug the hole.

Reiterating my stand on BMY, I am still bullish about the transformation underway and the ability to plug the gap. But this was intended to be a multi-year rerating process. Nothing has truly altered the risk profile materially to account for the valuation rerating other than an overall positive topline trend and strategic directional plans that could help close the gap.

Outlook for Q4

Given the long-term nature of my bullish outlook, I don't expect Q4 to throw up immediate risk-altering developments that could re-initiate Buys. I expect reported revenues and cash flows to be broadly stable. Eliquis should continue to post solid YoY growth supported by the Medicare Part D redesign and access improvements - implying near term strength but a larger shifting hole to be plugged eventually. Sequential normalization in Eliquis (despite the YoY improvements) appears to be the most balanced result that holds up the long term thesis best - so the extent of growth here will be crucial, as well as market reaction to that strength.

I also expect continued growth in assets like Camzyos, Breyanzi and Reblozyl which should reinforce revenue durability. However, they are not likely to be seen as catalysts as growth trends in these assets were already visible throughout 2025 (again trend confirmation versus new rerating catalyst).

I will closely watch progress in CELMoDs and CAR-T trial sequencing and commercialization as these are key areas now for the long term thesis and meaningful progress could support long term odds of plugging the upcoming patent cliffs. These developments should matter more now than quarterly headline number fluctuations, in my view.

Cost discipline and cash flow commentary will be relevant mainly for dividend comfort, not upside. And the room for absorbing pressures is sufficiently high enough to see any immediate shock there.

Overall, a clean Q4 with no negative surprises should be a non-event as far as rerating is concerned. On the contrary, any execution slippage in the long term growth plan could open up downsides - downsides that are unlikely to present an easy re-entry because it will challenge the long term growth thesis. The only opportunity for re-entry therefore could be valuation compression without an impact on the long term thesis. Until such opportunity arises, BMY should be a Hold - no fresh entries and booking partial profits on the faster than expected rerating in the past quarter.

data.title: Granite Point Mortgage: I'm Adding To My High-Yield Preferred Stock Position
data.description:
Introduction

Granite Point Mortgage Trust (GPMT) focuses on originating and investing in floating rate commercial mortgage loans. The company has gone through a difficult past few years, and in my recent articles on Granite Point, I focused on its preferred shares, as those, of course, rank senior to the common securities. As it has already been a while since my previous article was published, I thought this is a good moment to have another look at Granite Point, before it publishes its FY 2025 earnings.

The results: the situation should be improving

Although the company uses the ‘distributable earnings’ (hereinafter ‘DE’) as its main performance metric, the calculation of the DE uses the reported net income as its starting point. We are still waiting for Granite Point to report its full-year result, I wanted to have a look back at the 9M 2025 results to get a better idea of where the company may be going from here.

In the third quarter of 2025, Granite Point reported a total interest income of $33.7M, which is almost a quarter lower compared to the Q3 2024 interest income. That’s not necessarily bad news, as the total amount of interest expenses decreased by about a third. And that means the net interest income increased by just over a third on a YoY basis. An excellent performance on a YoY basis, but it’s also a substantial improvement from the weaker performance in the first semester.

The income statement above also shows the increased revenue from real estate owned operations. Unfortunately, this still doesn’t cover the total real estate related expenses, and it explains once again why Granite Point prefers to have its loans in good standing over repossessing assets.

In any case, the company reported a profit of $3M (as it was also able to benefit from a reversal of loan loss provisions). And while that sounds positive, keep in mind the quarterly preferred dividend payments are costing the company approximately $3.6M, which means the bottom line result was a net loss attributable to the common shareholders of GPMT of $0.6M.

But as mentioned earlier in this article, the net income is less relevant than the distributable earnings are. Unfortunately, this didn’t make a big difference for GPMT as its DE came in at just $0.85M before recording about $19.8M in write-offs. This means the third quarter was indeed pretty bad, but perhaps the company is on the mend.

During its Q3 conference call, the GPMT management mentioned that it continues to resolve asset issues while retiring the higher-cost debt. Some of the troubled loans were—surprisingly—resolved, and in one case, a student housing loan was resolved at over $3M above the carrying value of the asset.

Additionally, Granite Point Mortgage now expects to start to grow its portfolio again in 2026, but based on the management comments, we shouldn’t expect this to happen in a meaningful way before the summer. That being said, Granite Point continues to pay its quarterly dividend of $0.05 per share.

At the end of the third quarter, GPMT had a total asset base of $1.8B, backed by $1.22B in liabilities and $582M in equity. As there are 8.23M preferred shares outstanding, for a total of $206M. This means the common equity offers an additional cushion of approximately $375M.

And although the total amount of equity on the balance sheet has decreased in the first nine months of the year, the balance sheet has gotten safer. The total asset base shrank by $310M while the total amount of liabilities decreased by $272M. At the end of Q3, the average LTV ratio at origination stood at 65%, with about 31.2% of the loans having an LTV ratio that exceeded 70% at origination.

At the end of September, offices still represented almost 42% of the entire loan portfolio, a decrease of 410 bps compared to the end of 2024. Meanwhile, the exposure to multifamily real estate increased, and on its Q3 conference call, the GPMT management indicated the middle market loan segment is quite favorable for that type of asset.

The preferred shares: attractive as rate reset comes closer

As explained in a previous article, GPMT currently has just one series of preferred shares, the Series A preferred shares (GPMT.PR.A).

These preferred shares were issued in November 2021 and currently offer a preferred dividend of $1.75 per year for a preferred dividend yield of 7% based on the $25 principal value of the preferred shares. The call date is November 30 of this year , and from then on, the preferred dividend gets updated to the three-month SOFR rate plus a markup of 583 basis points. A second interesting feature is that there's a floor: the minimum preferred dividend will be 7%, even if the three-month SOFR would be zero.

At the current share price of $20.35, the current yield is approximately 8.6%. Meanwhile, the 3M SOFR stands at 3.63%, which means the preferred dividend would reset to 3.63% + 5.83% = 9.46%, indicating a preferred dividend of $2.36 per share per year. And based on the current share price, that would indicate a current yield of approximately 11.6%.

If the SOFR rate remains high, Granite Point should call the preferred shares. But it may not have the balance sheet strength to do so. In that case, preferred shareholders could be in for a very nice surprise for the first few floating rate dividends. And even if the 3M SOFR rate were to drop to, say, 2%, the preferred stock would still yield over 9% based on its current share price.

Investment thesis

While the reported book value is just under $8/share, I think the common shares of Granite Point Mortgage are a ‘speculative buy’ at best, and that’s why I’m using a ‘buy’ rating at this point. This could be a high-risk/high-reward speculative investment, but I think we may need a few additional quarters before being certain.

I also think the preferred shares are a ‘buy’ at this point. Yes, the economy and the sector are still a bit on shaky ground, but I like the more senior securities a bit better.

I currently have a long position in Granite Point’s preferred shares. I have no position in the common shares issued by Granite Point but I am considering initiating a [small] speculative long position.
